summary: The plan is largely well-scoped for a 2-week 3-4 person capstone, with deferrals explicitly flagged (DPP/MAP-Elites, Neo4j runtime, WebSockets, fine-tuning, self-evolving critics, production accounts). However several areas risk over-engineering for the timebox. The most consequential are: (1) the dual-track presentation of repo shape (a full 7-package apps+packages monorepo presented as primary, with the simpler folder-boundary option relegated to a one-line afterthought), (2) committing to BOTH idea subtypes with subtype-specific checks as a Goal rather than building one subtype end-to-end first, (3) the breadth of the model-gateway capability matrix and 6 model roles, and (4) the early Neo4j spike whose opportunity cost competes directly with the core demo loop. All four interact: each adds parallel surface area that a 3-engineer team cannot staff. None of these contradict locked decisions, but the draft under-specifies how to sequence/de-scope them under pressure, which is where overbuild creeps in. pgvector-vs-cosine and hosted-vs-local are already correctly framed with cheap fallbacks.

findings.finding: The repo shape is presented dual-track: §13 and §20 lead with a full apps/ + 7-package packages/ monorepo (contracts, runtime, model-gateway, scoring, persistence, observability, test-fixtures) as the primary scaffold, with the folder-boundary alternative demoted to a single trailing sentence ('For a smaller team, this can be one workspace... enforced by imports rather than separate publishable packages'). For a 3-4 person 2-week build, separate workspace packages add real cost (build wiring, cross-package versioning, dependency graph maintenance, slower iteration) for little MVP benefit since import-rule boundaries achieve the same architecture protection.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §13 (Shared Package / Config Strategy) and §20 (Repo Scaffold)
findings.proposed_fix: Invert the framing: make a single workspace with folder boundaries + import lint rules (the §4A Import Rules already specify these) the DEFAULT primary scaffold, and relegate the 7-package split to 'promote to packages only if a clean publish boundary is later needed.' Keep the same directory names as folders under apps/api and apps/web so the conceptual boundaries survive without the packaging tax.

findings.finding: Supporting BOTH candidate idea subtypes (cross_domain_transfer AND zeitgeist_synthesis) with subtype-specific checks is listed as a hard Goal, not a sequenced target. Each subtype needs its own check-runner adapters, critic mandate specialization, and demo-credible evidence; building both in parallel roughly doubles the verifier/check surface for a 2-week team whose core proof is 'later generations beat earlier ones,' which one subtype demonstrates just as well.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §1A Goals ('over both supported candidate idea types'), §2 (subtype check descriptions), §6 check-runners; locked decision: 'Canonical unit = CandidateIdea with two subtypes... sharing ONE lifecycle with subtype-specific checks'
findings.proposed_fix: The two-subtype lifecycle is LOCKED, so keep the shared lifecycle and the subtype enum. But explicitly sequence delivery: build ONE subtype (recommend cross_domain_transfer — its checks like source-domain validity / toy executable checks are more objective and demo-credible than zeitgeist timing/signal-grounding) end-to-end first, with the second subtype's checks allowed to return CheckResult 'skipped' (the §6 mechanism already exists) until the first is demo-solid. Add this as an explicit build-order note in §2/§18 so a 'skipped' second subtype is a planned milestone, not a gap.

findings.finding: The early Neo4j lineage-analysis spike is a locked-required early task (testing ancestry, winner-contribution, lineage-distance/diversity queries, dashboard export shape), but it competes directly for engineer-time with the core React Flow lineage dashboard, which is fed by the SAME storage-agnostic projection and IS part of the demo proof. Spiking a deferred database before the demo loop is stable is a classic opportunity-cost trap for a 2-week build.
findings.evidence: CONSTRAINTS.md Graph Technology ('early Neo4j spike that tests ancestry queries... dashboard export shape'); DECISIONS.md ADR-007 ('Early Neo4j lineage-analysis spike is required'); ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §18 Required early spikes
findings.proposed_fix: Keep the spike (locked) but time-box and de-risk it explicitly: schedule it AFTER the LineageGraphProjection + React Flow demo path is working, cap it at ~1 engineer-day, and define its success as a throwaway notebook proving query shapes — NOT a synced read model. Make explicit in §18 that the spike must never block the demo loop and produces no runtime dependency. Recommend it run in the second week as a stretch validation, not week-one foundational work.

findings.finding: The ModelGateway defines six model roles (population_generator, critic, subtype_check, embedding, final_judge, fusion_synthesis) plus a full per-route capability matrix (structuredOutputs, toolCalling, embeddings, streaming) and a fallbackRouteIds chain. The capability-matrix normalization across providers is called out in ADR-004 Tradeoffs as significant work. For MVP this is more provider-abstraction machinery than a single-primary-provider demo needs day one.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §9 Model Registry (ModelRole, ModelRoute, capabilities); DECISIONS.md ADR-004 Tradeoffs ('adapter capability matrix adds work')
findings.proposed_fix: Provider-agnostic gateway is LOCKED and the role enum is cheap to keep. But start with OpenRouter-only routes for all roles and a minimal capability matrix (structuredOutputs + embeddings only — the two that actually gate the demo per ADR-004), deferring toolCalling/streaming capability fields and the multi-hop fallbackRouteIds chain until a second provider is actually wired. The ADR-004 fallback ('ship with OpenRouter only behind the gateway if time is tight') already authorizes this; make it the default plan, not the contingency.

findings.finding: §10 mentions both in-process workers AND an optional queue, and §12 lists optional Neo4j export among indexer strategies. The testing list (§14) is also broad (13 must-have tests plus 4 demo rehearsals) for a 2-week build. There is mild risk of teams reading the 'optional queue' and full test matrix as expected scope.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §10 ('A queue can be added if needed'), §12 (optional Neo4j export), §14 Testing Strategy
findings.proposed_fix: Explicitly state in §10 that in-process workers are the MVP target and a queue is NOT in scope unless concurrency proves it necessary (the text leans this way but should be unambiguous). Mark the §14 list with a minimal must-pass subset (cap enforcement, event append/replay reconstruction, structured-output validation, novelty fixture) vs. nice-to-have, so the safety-critical tests are clearly the non-negotiable floor and the rest are aspirational.

findings.finding: Hosted + local dual deployment is locked, but for a single in-room June 29 showcase the hosted path adds deploy/config/access-control verification surface (thin access control is itself an open question in DECISIONS.md) that may not earn its cost if the demo is presented locally. The plan correctly makes local-first the reliability winner, but does not state whether hosted is actually being pursued for this showcase or kept purely as a future-proofing seam.
findings.evidence: DECISIONS.md ADR-009 (local-first plus hosted, locked) and Remaining Open Verification ('Thin access control for hosted showcase'); ASSUMPTIONS.md A-002 (local-first reliability wins)
findings.proposed_fix: Decision needed: is hosted deployment a committed deliverable for the Jun 29 showcase or a deferred-if-time stretch? Recommended default: treat hosted as an explicit STRETCH for this showcase (present locally), keeping the architecture deployment-agnostic but spending zero week-one time on hosting/access-control. This respects the locked 'support both' decision (the seam stays) while removing deploy work from the critical path. Confirm with the human whether reviewers require a shareable hosted URL.
